title: mrmopt Package — Project Memory
text: mrmopt (Media Response Modeling Optimization) R package Ben Denis Shaffer Bayesian nonlinear media response modeling. fits saturation/diminishing-returns curves media spend vs. KPI data using brms (Stan backend), quantifies uncertainty via posterior distributions, supports media mix optimization via nloptr. Version: 0.1.0 License: MIT Docs: https://bdshaff.github.io/mrmopt/

Added S3method(plot,opt_mix_result) NAMESPACE. Fixed vignette error hierarchical_curves.Rmd:218 plot(opt_post, type = "posterior") falling plot.default(). Fixed: (1) rewrote Marginal Return section response_curve_theory.Rmd correct characterization new figure showing MR vs |b|; (2) mrm_summary.R mrm_summary_hier.R now detect interior MR peaks data (.max(mr)) rather assuming log-form curves lack one; (3) added tests test-hlpr_summary_core.R |b| > 1 (peak) |b| ≤ 1 (peak) cases Vignette build strategy: Stan compilation vignette rendering causes rstan/rmarkdown sink() incompatibility, making impossible build vignettes full code execution standard R CMD build. title: mrmopt Package — Project Memory
code: # 1. Fit fit <- fit_response(   data    = my_data,   spend   = "ad_spend",   kpi     = "conversions",   date    = "week",   units   = "impressions",   # optional   type    = "gompertz",   midpoint_range = c(0.1, 0.5),   ceiling_max    = 3 )  # 2. Inspect print(fit) plot(fit)            # dashboard: response + AR/MR + cost-per plot(fit, type = "diagnostics")  # trace plots + PPCs  # 3. Analyze mrm_params(fit) mrm_summary(fit)   # 4. Compare models mrm_plot_compare(list(gompertz = fit_g, logistic = fit_l), layout = "overlay")  # 5. Optimize (point estimate — fast, single solution) opt <- opt_mix(list(ch1 = fit1, ch2 = fit2), budget = 500000) print(opt)           # formatted console summary (calls summary()) summary(opt)         # same formatted output opt_table(opt)       # tidy tibble with per-channel deltas (current vs optimal)  # 5b. Optimize (posterior — distribution of solutions) opt_post <- opt_mix(list(ch1 = fit1, ch2 = fit2),                     method = "posterior", budget = 500000, n_draws = 200) plot(opt_post, type = "posterior")  # 5c. Period budgets (e.g., $10M annual) opt_annual <- opt_mix(models, budget = 10000000, n_weeks = 52)  # 6. Visualize on response curves plot(opt, type = "curves")     # response curves with current + optimal points plot(opt, type = "returns")    # AR/MR curves with current + optimal points  # 7. Compare two optimization results comp <- compare(opt, opt_post) plot(comp, type = "spend")     # dumbbell chart summary(opt)                   # tidy comparison tibble with deltas

title: mrmopt Package — Project Memory
text: Single function two methods: - method = "point" (default): Uses posterior median parameters → single nloptr solve (~1s) - method = "posterior": Optimizes N posterior draws → distribution solutions (~6s 200 draws × 9 channels)

title: mrmopt Package — Project Memory
text: posterior path uses raw parameter draws + rm_dispatch() instead brms::posterior_epred() — 10,000x speedup makes posterior optimization practical.
code: opt_mix() ├─ hlpr_auto_constraints() / hlpr_parse_constraints()  # constraint setup ├─ opt_mix_point()          # point-estimate path │  ├─ mrm_response_function()  # extract median curve │  └─ hlpr_opt_solve()         # nloptr COBYLA └─ opt_mix_posterior()      # posterior path    ├─ hlpr_extract_draws()     # pre-extract & unscale all draws (fast path)    ├─ make_draw_objective()    # build objective from raw draws + rm_dispatch    └─ hlpr_opt_solve() × N    # one solve per draw (optionally parallel)

text: opt_mix API redesign: summary.opt_mix_result() now produces formatted console output (previously done print). print.opt_mix_result() thin wrapper calling summary(). opt_table() new plain exported function returns tidy comparison tibble (previously returned summary()). internal plot_opt_* helpers now standalone exported functions named opt_plot_allocation(), opt_plot_comparison(), opt_plot_posterior(), opt_plot_curves(), opt_plot_returns(), opt_plot_compare(). plot.opt_mix_result() plot.opt_mix_compare() thin dispatchers calling opt_plot_* functions. opt_plot_posterior() now hard-errors (instead message + fallback) called point result. compare() function: S3 generic + method side--side diff two opt_mix_result objects plot.opt_mix_compare dumbbell chart. Response curve overlay plots: plot(opt, type = "curves") shows response curves current + optimal points; plot(opt, type = "returns") shows AR/MR curves positions. Uses hlpr_opt_metrics() interpolation. mrms stored result: opt_mix() now stores model list result curve/returns plots can access response data. opt_mix() rewrite: Two-layer architecture method = "point" (fast, single solution) method = "posterior" (distribution solutions via raw posterior draws — 10,000x faster posterior_epred()). Unified return structure, S3 class opt_mix_result print, plot, summary methods. Budget/n_weeks support period-level optimization. Constraint system: User-supplied constraints data frame absolute bounds (min_spend/max_spend), share-based bounds (min_share/max_share), fixed channels (fixed = TRUE). hlpr_build_solution(): Shared builder unified solution tibble current-state metrics, optimal units (static CPU), response rates, CIs, shares. hlpr_extract_draws(): Pre-extracts unscales posterior draws fitted models fast optimization loop evaluation. anchor_strength prior: Replaced synthetic (0,0) anchor point injection anchor_strength mrmopt_prior() — prior-based floor constraint works uniformly across 6 curve types. anchor_zero deprecated. Trace plot labels: Fixed strip label truncation — renamed mcmc.list columns short labels (b, c, d, e) passing bayesplot::mcmc_trace() R/plot.mrmfit.R date_range metadata: Stored c(min(date), max(date)) fitted models fit_response() mrm_plot_compare() label collision fix: comparing -channel/-type models across time periods, appends short date range "Mon 'YY–Mon 'YY"; respects user-supplied names(models)

title: Roadmap
text: New function: fit_response_hier() — shipped. See Hierarchical Response Curves article worked example, design/fit_response_hier.md design. Media channels rarely homogeneous. TV spend spans broadcast, cable, streaming — different audience reach response dynamics. Social covers multiple partners, tactics, creatives. Standard response curve modeling treats channel single unit, either overfits sparse sub-channel data discards granularity altogether. fit_response_hier() fits single hierarchical model one channel curve parameters partially pooled across sub-channel groupings: fixed effects represent channel-level mean curve. Random effects level (subtype → station) drawn level , sparse units (e.g., small cable station handful weeks) borrow strength better-identified peers. degree shrinkage automatic data-driven — units observations get pulled less toward group mean. Delivered: Channel-level, sub-type-level, unit-level curves single model (mrm_summary_hier(), mrm_infer_hier()) six curve forms, including log-based forms (midpoint reparameterized internally log scale sampling stability) Arbitrary-depth nested hierarchies Posterior uncertainty correctly reflects data sparsity level Optimization level hierarchy via as_mrmfit_list() + opt_mix() Per-unit shrinkage visualizations via mrm_plot_hier() Pooling operates curve shape parameters (b, e); scale (d) allowed vary freely reflect size differences across units
code: fit_tv <- fit_response_hier(   data      = tv_data,   spend     = "spend",   kpi       = "conversions",   date      = "week",   group     = c("subtype", "station"),   # nested hierarchy (any depth)   type      = "gompertz" )

title: Roadmap
text: New function: fit_response_synergy() Standard media response modeling assumes channels contribute independently additively KPI. simplifying assumption MMM tools carry forward without question. practice, simultaneous investment across channels may produce efficiency gains (losses) purely additive model misses. fit_response_synergy() fits joint model across channels pairwise interaction terms — one per channel pair — capture residual co-efficiency signals beyond individual response curves predict: 9 channels, 36 possible pairwise interaction terms. prevent overfitting handle near-certain multicollinearity channels, interaction terms regularized horseshoe prior — sparsity-inducing prior shrinks interactions toward zero allowing small number well-identified interactions remain. Important framing: Interaction terms estimated attributed KPI volumes interpreted predictive efficiency corrections, causal synergies. upstream attribution model produced attributed volumes already assumed channel independence. terms capture residual signal data useful optimization, structural causal claim. Key properties: Per-channel nonlinear response curves (Hill / Log-Logistic parameterization) Horseshoe-regularized pairwise interaction terms Collinearity diagnostics per channel pair — warns interaction likely unidentifiable due correlated spend patterns Interaction terms opt-; default behavior remains independent channels
code: fit_syn <- fit_response_synergy(   models = list(tv = fit_tv, social = fit_social, search = fit_search, ...),   data   = joint_data )

title: Roadmap
text: Estimated geometric adstock decay integrated fit_response() via optional adstock = TRUE argument. decay parameter estimated jointly saturation curve parameters carry full posterior uncertainty, consistent Meridian’s approach. Default prior: Uniform(0, 1) decay parameter — uninformative, consistent Meridian’s defaults. Users can tighten domain knowledge carryover windows specific channels.
code: fit <- fit_response(   data    = my_data,   spend   = "tv_spend",   kpi     = "conversions",   date    = "week",   type    = "gompertz",   adstock = TRUE   # estimate decay parameter jointly )

title: Roadmap
text: mrmopt currently operates response curve layer — fits saturation curves attributed KPI volumes produced upstream MMM. valid practical workflow, means saturation parameters estimated data already implicit independence linearity assumption baked attribution model. ground-joint MMM estimate adstock decay, saturation curve parameters, channel contribution coefficients simultaneously raw spend total KPI data — approach taken Google Meridian PyMC-Marketing. implemented via hand-written Stan model using CmdStanR backend, giving full control model structure without constraints brms formula interface. Priors aligned Meridian’s published defaults (ec ~ TruncatedNormal(0.8, 0.8, 0.1, 10) normalized spend, decay ~ Uniform(0, 1), beta ~ HalfNormal(5)). Key design: new fit_mmm() function returning mrmfit_joint S3 class feeds existing opt_mix() optimization infrastructure via draw-matrix interface used posterior optimization path. substantial modeling engineering undertaking developed incrementally. Single-market models without geo-level hierarchy initial target scope.

what: Where mrmopt sits in the analytics stack
title: Getting Started
text: mrmopt response curve optimization layer. takes weekly spend attributed KPI data channel — kind output produced upstream Media Mix Model Multi-Touch Attribution model — adds three things upstream model typically doesn’t provide: Nonlinear saturation curves — explicitly modeling diminishing returns rather assuming linear relationship spend KPI Bayesian uncertainty — credible intervals curves, marginal returns, cost-per-unit honestly reflect data doesn’t tell Uncertainty-aware optimization — budget allocation accounts parameter uncertainty rather optimizing single point estimate mrmopt replace joint MMM. operates downstream one.

title: Getting Started
text: Every analysis mrmopt follows iterative loop: two loops — convergence checking curve type selection — parts new users often skip. Skipping produces unreliable curves misleading optimization results. vignette shows look step.
code: Raw data   └─► fit_response()          # Fit one channel, one curve type         └─► mrm_plot_diagnostics()  # Check convergence               └─► mrms_plot_compare()  # Compare curve types                     └─► fit_response()  # Best type, full iterations, all channels                           └─► opt_mix()  # Optimize the portfolio                                 └─► opt_summary() / opt_plot_*()

title: Getting Started
text: mrmopt_data built-dataset five media channels, 104 weeks (two years) weekly spend attributed conversions. Required format: One row per channel-week. minimum need date, spend, kpi columns. optional units column (impressions, GRPs, clicks) unlocks cost-per-unit metrics. Channels modeled one time — filter single channel calling fit_response().

title: Getting Started
text: Start one channel one curve type. Gompertz reasonable default channels concave diminishing returns start. fit_response() fits Bayesian nonlinear model using Stan via brms. midpoint_range ceiling_max scale-invariant prior controls — need specify raw Stan priors unless want .
code: ps_data <- mrmopt_data |> filter(channel == "Paid Search")  fit_ps <- fit_response(   data           = ps_data,   spend          = "spend",   kpi            = "conversions",   date           = "week",   type           = "gompertz",   midpoint_range = c(0.1, 0.5),   ceiling_max    = 3,   refresh        = 0,   iter = 1000,   chains = 2 ) #> conversions ~ c + (d - c) * exp(-exp(b * (spend - e)))  #> b ~ 1 #> c ~ 1 #> d ~ 1 #> e ~ 1

title: Getting Started
text: print() gives five-section summary fitted model: Reading top bottom: Response Curve Summary — curve type channel/KPI columns used Current Performance — channel sits right now: weekly spend, KPI, cost-per-conversion, average return (AR), marginal return (MR) Parameters — four fitted curve parameters. d (ceiling) e (midpoint) interpretable: ceiling maximum predicted KPI, midpoint spend level curve steepest Response Curve Summary — three reference points curve anchored MR multiples, helping see much room left grow quickly returns declining Bayes R² — posterior distribution explained variance. Higher better; values 0.4 suggest curve may well-identified data mrm_params() gives focused table just four parameters:

title: Getting Started
text: interpreting anything, confirm model converged.  Two things check: Trace plots — four chains mix freely (overlap completely, trends drifts). chains separating stuck, model convergence problems. Posterior predictive check — blue posterior draws envelope black observed data line. systematic gap means curve form misspecified. numerical indicators target Rhat ≤ 1.01 Bulk ESS > 400 parameters. printed mrm_summary(): convergence poor, try increasing iter warmup, adjust priors tighter midpoint_range.
code: mrm_plot_diagnostics(fit_ps) summary(fit_ps) #>  Family: gaussian  #>   Links: mu = identity  #> Formula: conversions ~ c + (d - c) * exp(-exp(b * (spend - e)))  #>          b ~ 1 #>          c ~ 1 #>          d ~ 1 #>          e ~ 1 #>    Data: data (Number of observations: 104)  #>   Draws: 2 chains, each with iter = 1000; warmup = 500; thin = 1; #>          total post-warmup draws = 1000 #>  #> Regression Coefficients: #>             Estimate Est.Error l-95% CI u-95% CI Rhat Bulk_ESS Tail_ESS #> b_Intercept    -9.73      0.20    -9.99    -9.24 1.00      346      336 #> c_Intercept     0.03      0.01     0.02     0.05 1.00      399      525 #> d_Intercept     0.99      0.01     0.97     1.00 1.00      452      562 #> e_Intercept     0.47      0.00     0.46     0.47 1.00      449      385 #>  #> Further Distributional Parameters: #>       Estimate Est.Error l-95% CI u-95% CI Rhat Bulk_ESS Tail_ESS #> sigma     0.03      0.00     0.02     0.03 1.00      732      722 #>  #> Draws were sampled using sampling(NUTS). For each parameter, Bulk_ESS #> and Tail_ESS are effective sample size measures, and Rhat is the potential #> scale reduction factor on split chains (at convergence, Rhat = 1).

title: Getting Started
text: convergence confirmed, look curve :  dashboard shows three panels: Response curve — KPI vs. spend posterior credible band. current operating point marked. Average & marginal return — AR MR curves. MR well AR, channel diminishing returns zone. MR ≈ AR, underinvested. Cost-per — cost-per-conversion across spend range. Flat declining cost-per means channel absorbs spend efficiently.
code: mrm_plot(fit_ps)

title: Getting Started
text: mrmopt supports six curve forms. right choice depends data — channels S-shaped response (low returns low spend, peak efficiency middle, diminishing returns high spend) others show concave diminishing returns first dollar. Fit second type compare visually:  Prefer curve : tighter credible bands (better identified data) higher Bayes R² Shows systematic gap predictive band data diagnostic PPC full comparison across six types, see Diagnostics & Comparison vignette.
code: fit_ps_ll <- fit_response(   data           = ps_data,   spend          = "spend",   kpi            = "conversions",   date           = "week",   type           = "log_logistic",   midpoint_range = c(0.1, 0.5),   ceiling_max    = 3,   refresh        = 0,   iter = 1000,   chains = 2 ) #> conversions ~ c + ((d - c)/(1 + exp(b * (log(spend) - log(e)))))  #> b ~ 1 #> c ~ 1 #> d ~ 1 #> e ~ 1 mrms_plot_compare(   list(gompertz = fit_ps, log_logistic = fit_ps_ll),   interval = "predictio" )

title: Response Curve Theory and Data Requirements
text: Media channels deliver returns proportion spend. first dollar spent channel typically efficient — reaches responsive audience, captures highest-intent search queries, occupies cheapest inventory. spend increases, additional dollar reaches progressively less responsive audiences, drives incrementally smaller lifts, encounters diminishing returns. behaviour well-described S-shaped saturation curve: response rises steeply first, passes inflection point, gradually levels toward asymptotic ceiling. shape encodes two important economic realities: Diminishing marginal returns — incremental KPI generated additional dollar spend falls total spend grows. capacity addressable market ceiling — many consumers market, much demand capture. Linear models represent either property. response curve model fits nonlinear, parametric relationship spend KPI captures .

title: Response Curve Theory and Data Requirements
text: Three spend levels partition curve interpretable zones: Range min (peak MR): spend level dollar efficient. Spending level means leaving efficiency table. Range max (MR decay threshold): spend level marginal return declined specified fraction peak (default 70%). Beyond , diminishing returns accelerate meaningfully. Gompertz curve annotated three operating range points.
code: x_range <- seq(0, 120000, length.out = 1000) y_gompertz <- mrmopt:::rm_Gompertz(x_range, b = -0.00005, c = 50, d = 900, e = 50000) mr_g <- c(NA, diff(y_gompertz))  peak_mr_x  <- x_range[which.max(mr_g)] decay_mr   <- 0.7 * max(mr_g, na.rm = TRUE) decay_x    <- x_range[which(mr_g <= decay_mr & x_range > peak_mr_x)[1]]  tibble(x = x_range, y = y_gompertz) |>   ggplot(aes(x, y)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 1, colour = "#2d6fa6") +   geom_vline(xintercept = peak_mr_x, linetype = "dashed", colour = "#e07b39") +   geom_vline(xintercept = decay_x,   linetype = "dashed", colour = "#c94040") +   annotate("text", x = peak_mr_x, y = 150, label = "Peak MR\n(range min)",            hjust = -0.1, size = 3, colour = "#e07b39") +   annotate("text", x = decay_x,   y = 350, label = "70% MR decay\n(range max)",            hjust = -0.1, size = 3, colour = "#c94040") +   scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::dollar_format(scale = 1e-3, suffix = "k")) +   labs(x = "Weekly Spend", y = "Conversions") +   theme_minimal()

title: Response Curve Theory and Data Requirements
text: model assumes single, stable response curve across entire observed period. Structural breaks — major change audience targeting, shift channel strategy, platform algorithm change — violate assumption. Fitting break produces curve represents neither period well. mrmopt provides two approaches handling within-channel structural variation: Hierarchical models via fit_response_hier(): channel natural grouping structure (e.g., creative variants, audience segments, partner stations), hierarchical model fits shared curve shape group-level deviations. borrows strength across groups allowing effective saturation behaviour. See Hierarchical Curves vignette details. Rolling-window models via fit_response_window() (planned): channels response curve drifts time — due seasonality, competitive dynamics, platform changes — rolling-window approach fits separate curves overlapping time windows, tracking parameters evolve. development roadmap. breaks suspected neither approach applies, fit separate models sub-period. date_range metadata stored fitted model (accessible fit$date_range) makes straightforward compare models across periods using mrm_plot_compare().

title: Response Curve Theory and Data Requirements
text: six response curves mrmopt share unified four-parameter structure. key distinctions : Symmetry: Logistic Log-Logistic symmetric around inflection point; Gompertz family asymmetric. Scale: Linear-scale curves (Logistic, Gompertz, Reflected Gompertz) always interior marginal return peak. Log-scale curves (Log-Logistic, Weibull, Reflected Weibull) interior MR peak |b| > 1 monotonically decreasing MR |b| ≤ 1. Inflection timing: Within scale group, standard, Gompertz, reflected Gompertz variants place inflection 50%, ~37%, ~63% response range respectively. Fitting begins fit_response(). next vignette covers full fitting analysis workflow using realistic simulated dataset.

title: Roadmap
text: New function: fit_response_hier() — available now. See Hierarchical Response Curves article worked example. Media channels rarely homogeneous. TV spend spans broadcast, cable, streaming — different audience reach response dynamics. Social covers multiple partners, tactics, creatives. Standard response curve modeling treats channel single unit, either overfits sparse sub-channel data discards granularity altogether. fit_response_hier() fits single hierarchical model one channel curve parameters partially pooled across sub-channel groupings: fixed effects represent channel-level mean curve. Random effects level (subtype → station) drawn level , sparse units (e.g., small cable station handful weeks) borrow strength better-identified peers. degree shrinkage automatic data-driven — units observations get pulled less toward group mean. Delivered: Channel-level, sub-type-level, unit-level curves single model (mrm_summary_hier(), mrm_infer_hier()) six curve forms, including log-based forms (midpoint reparameterized internally log scale sampling stability) Arbitrary-depth nested hierarchies Posterior uncertainty correctly reflects data sparsity level Optimization level hierarchy via as_mrmfit_list() + opt_mix() Per-unit shrinkage visualizations via mrm_plot_hier() Pooling operates curve shape parameters (b, e); scale (d) allowed vary freely reflect size differences across units
code: fit_tv <- fit_response_hier(   data      = tv_data,   spend     = "spend",   kpi       = "conversions",   date      = "week",   group     = c("subtype", "station"),   # nested hierarchy (any depth)   type      = "gompertz" )

title: Roadmap
text: New function: mrm_rolling() Standard response curve fitting treats full observation window single stationary period. practice, relationship spend KPI shifts time — seasonal demand, competitive pressure, creative fatigue cause effective ceiling saturation point vary. mrm_rolling() makes drift visible fitting sequence response curves across overlapping windows data. window fit independently using fit_response(), producing list mrmfit objects anchored respective date ranges. companion plot shows parameter’s posterior median credible interval evolves across windows, making seasonal drift ceiling (d) midpoint (e) directly visible. Key properties: new model architecture — window calls fit_response() directly window_size controls timescale local estimation; stride controls resolution vs. compute cost Returns named list mrmfit objects compatible mrms_plot_compare() dedicated mrm_plot_rolling() show parameter evolution time credible ribbons Note: diagnostic exploratory tool. windows fit independently formal pooling across — adjacent windows borrow strength . longer-term time-varying parameter model (see ) addresses limitation principled shared estimation framework.
code: rolling_fits <- mrm_rolling(   data        = channel_data,   spend       = "spend",   kpi         = "conversions",   date        = "week",   type        = "gompertz",   window_size = 13,   # weeks per window   stride      = 4     # weeks between window starts )

title: Roadmap
text: New function: fit_response_synergy() Standard media response modeling assumes channels contribute independently additively KPI. simplifying assumption MMM tools carry forward without question. practice, simultaneous investment across channels may produce efficiency gains (losses) purely additive model misses. fit_response_synergy() fits joint model across channels pairwise interaction terms — one per channel pair — capture residual co-efficiency signals beyond individual response curves predict: 9 channels, 36 possible pairwise interaction terms. prevent overfitting handle near-certain multicollinearity channels, interaction terms regularized horseshoe prior — sparsity-inducing prior shrinks interactions toward zero allowing small number well-identified interactions remain. Important framing: Interaction terms estimated attributed KPI volumes interpreted predictive efficiency corrections, causal synergies. upstream attribution model produced attributed volumes already assumed channel independence. terms capture residual signal data useful optimization, structural causal claim. Key properties: Per-channel nonlinear response curves (Hill / Log-Logistic parameterization) Horseshoe-regularized pairwise interaction terms Collinearity diagnostics per channel pair — warns interaction likely unidentifiable due correlated spend patterns Interaction terms opt-; default behavior remains independent channels
code: fit_syn <- fit_response_synergy(   models = list(tv = fit_tv, social = fit_social, search = fit_search, ...),   data   = joint_data )

title: Roadmap

title: Roadmap
text: New function: fit_response_tv() mrm_rolling() makes parameter drift visible estimates window independently. proper formulation treats curve parameters functions time — continuous, smooth, estimated jointly across observations single model. time-varying parameter model, d (ceiling) e (midpoint) follow random walk Gaussian Process time: dt∼𝒩(dt−1,σd),et∼𝒩(et−1,σe)d_t \sim \mathcal{N}(d_{t-1},\ \sigma_d), \qquad e_t \sim \mathcal{N}(e_{t-1},\ \sigma_e) evolution variance (σ_d, σ_e) estimated data controls timescale parameter change — small σ means near-stationary parameters; larger σ allows rapid seasonal swings. Parameters b (steepness) c (floor) held fixed channel level, reflect structural properties channel rather seasonal demand. Relationship rolling window approach: window size mrm_rolling() discrete proxy GP kernel bandwidth . 13-week window implies parameters change ~quarterly timescale — equivalent GP kernel length-scale ~13 weeks. time-varying model captures continuously without boundary artifacts window edges. Relationship seasonal hierarchical models: simpler intermediate formulation — year-level partial pooling d alone, fit via brms multilevel machinery — worth building first stepping stone. addresses year--year ceiling shifts (e.g., growing market) without requiring custom Stan code, output directly compatible existing opt_mix() infrastructure. Key properties: evolution = "random_walk": AR(1) prior parameters — tractable Stan, natural slowly drifting seasonality evolution = "gp": Gaussian Process prior — flexible, kernel bandwidth estimated data, significantly expensive Requires hand-written Stan model via CmdStanR — outside brms formula interface Returns mrmfit_tv object time-indexed parameter posteriors mrm_plot_tv() visualization showing evolving curve across observation window
code: fit_tv <- fit_response_tv(   data       = channel_data,   spend      = "spend",   kpi        = "conversions",   date       = "week",   type       = "gompertz",   varying    = c("d", "e"),   # which parameters evolve over time   evolution  = "random_walk"  # or "gp" for Gaussian Process )

title: Roadmap
text: mrmopt currently operates response curve layer — fits saturation curves attributed KPI volumes produced upstream MMM. valid practical workflow, means saturation parameters estimated data already implicit independence linearity assumption baked attribution model. ground-joint MMM estimate adstock decay, saturation curve parameters, channel contribution coefficients simultaneously raw spend total KPI data — approach taken Google Meridian PyMC-Marketing. implemented via hand-written Stan model using CmdStanR backend, giving full control model structure without constraints brms formula interface. Priors aligned Meridian’s published defaults (ec ~ TruncatedNormal(0.8, 0.8, 0.1, 10) normalized spend, decay ~ Uniform(0, 1), beta ~ HalfNormal(5)). Key design: new fit_mmm() function returning mrmfit_joint S3 class feeds existing opt_mix() optimization infrastructure via draw-matrix interface used posterior optimization path. substantial modeling engineering undertaking developed incrementally. Single-market models without geo-level hierarchy initial target scope.

title: mrmopt
code: library(mrmopt)  # Built-in example data: 5 channels, 104 weeks each data(mrmopt_data)  # 1. Fit a response curve for a single channel paid_search <- mrmopt_data[mrmopt_data$channel == "Paid Search", ]  fit <- fit_response(   data           = paid_search,   spend          = "spend",   kpi            = "conversions",   date           = "week",   type           = "gompertz",   midpoint_range = c(0.1, 0.5),   ceiling_max    = 3 )  # 2. Inspect and visualize print(fit)                       # performance summary, parameters, Bayes R² mrm_plot(fit)                    # response curve + AR/MR + cost-per dashboard mrm_plot_diagnostics(fit)        # trace plots and posterior predictive checks  # 3. Optimize across a portfolio of fitted channels opt <- opt_mix(   list(     paid_search  = fit_ps,     paid_social  = fit_soc,     display      = fit_dis,     online_video = fit_ov,     tv           = fit_tv   ),   budget = 500000 )  opt_summary(opt)                 # formatted allocation summary opt_table(opt)                   # tidy tibble of current vs. optimal deltas opt_plot_allocation(opt)         # grouped bar: current vs. optimal spend opt_plot_curves(opt)             # response curves with optimal spend marked

title: mrmopt
text: 6 response curve types — Logistic, Gompertz, Reflected Gompertz, Log-Logistic, Weibull, Reflected Weibull, covering concave S-shaped diminishing returns Full posterior uncertainty — credible intervals curves, marginal returns, average returns, cost-per-unit every spend level Smart prior automation — scale-invariant prior specification via midpoint_range ceiling_max; manual Stan required Model diagnostics comparison — trace plots, posterior predictive checks, overlay plots comparing curve types time periods via mrms_plot_compare() Portfolio optimization — point-estimate (fast, ~1s) posterior (distributional, ~6s 200 draws) optimization via opt_mix(), flexible constraint specification including absolute bounds, share constraints, fixed channels Explicit analysis functions — opt_summary(), opt_table(), opt_plot_allocation(), opt_plot_comparison(), opt_plot_curves(), opt_plot_returns(), opt_plot_posterior() expose output directly rather hiding behind single dispatcher Period budgeting — optimize planning horizon (e.g., annual $10M broken weekly allocations via n_weeks)

title: Fit a within-channel hierarchical response curve model using brms — fit_response_hier
text: hierarchical counterpart fit_response follows conventions: global data scaling, b/c/ d/e parameterization, prior-specification tiers. Prior specification follows three tiers fit_response (automatic, simplified via midpoint_range/ceiling_max/ floor_min, manual raw brmsprior). population priors identical single-fit case; partial-pooling SD priors added automatically (see hlpr_resolve_prior_hier). log-based forms ("log_logistic", "weibull", "reflected_weibull") midpoint enters log(e), requires e > 0. Group-level deviations e can violate produce NaN sampling, midpoint reparameterized internally log scale (unconstrained parameter le = log(e) replaces log(e) model). transparent: le translated back e extraction, outputs (params_hier, response_df, summaries, plots) report e original spend units.

title: Define a response form for a nonlinear model — hlpr_define_response_form
text: function supports following response forms: logistic: \(y = c + ((d - c)/(1 + exp(b*(x - e))))\) log_logistic: \(y = c + ((d - c)/(1 + exp(b*(log(x) - log(e))))\) gompertz: \(y = c + (d - c) * exp(-exp(b * (x - e)))\) reflected_gompertz: \(y = c + (d - c) * (1 - exp(-exp(b * (-x + e))))\) weibull: \(y = c + (d - c) * exp(-exp(b * (log(x) - log(e))))\) reflected_weibull: \(y = c + (d - c) * (1 - exp(-exp(b * (-log(x) + log(e))))\)

title: Create a prior specification for response curve fitting — mrmopt_prior
text: midpoint_range two-element numeric vector specifying lower upper bounds midpoint (inflection point) fractions x-axis range. Values 0 1. example, `c(0.1, 0.9)` means midpoint expected fall 10th 90th percentile x range. Default `c(0.1, 0.9)`. ceiling_max multiplier observed maximum response variable, defining upper bound upper asymptote (ceiling). example, `ceiling_max = 3` means ceiling can 3 times observed max y. Must >= 1. Default `5`. floor_min scalar specifying lower bound lower asymptote (floor), original data units. Default `0`, meaning response go zero. anchor_strength single positive numeric value controlling tightly floor parameter (`c`) constrained around `floor_min`. Expressed fraction observed y range: `0.05` means prior SD `c` equals 5% observed y range scaled space. Smaller values produce narrower credible intervals low spend. Set `NULL` loose behavior (broad prior, minimal constraint floor). Default `0.05`.

title: Create a prior specification for response curve fitting — mrmopt_prior
text: response curve model four parameters: floor (c) Lower asymptote — minimum response value. ceiling (d) Upper asymptote — maximum response value. steepness (b) Growth rate controlling sharply curve rises. midpoint (e) Inflection point — curve reaches half range. function allows set constraints midpoint ceiling, parameters users typically intuition . Steepness managed internally broad, scale-aware defaults. floor defaults zero can overridden models non-zero baselines. full control, can bypass interface pass raw prior object directly fit_response.

title: Create a prior specification for response curve fitting — mrmopt_prior
code: # Default priors mrmopt_prior() #> mrm_prior specification: #>   midpoint range : [0.1, 0.9] (x-axis fraction) #>   ceiling max    : 5 x observed max of y #>   floor min      : 0 (original data units) #>   anchor strength: 0.05 (fraction of y range)  # Expect midpoint in the first half of the x range, ceiling up to 2x observed max mrmopt_prior(midpoint_range = c(0.05, 0.5), ceiling_max = 2) #> mrm_prior specification: #>   midpoint range : [0.05, 0.5] (x-axis fraction) #>   ceiling max    : 2 x observed max of y #>   floor min      : 0 (original data units) #>   anchor strength: 0.05 (fraction of y range)  # Allow a non-zero floor mrmopt_prior(floor_min = 100) #> mrm_prior specification: #>   midpoint range : [0.1, 0.9] (x-axis fraction) #>   ceiling max    : 5 x observed max of y #>   floor min      : 100 (original data units) #>   anchor strength: 0.05 (fraction of y range)

what: Optimize media mix allocation across channels — opt_mix
title: Optimize media mix allocation across channels — opt_mix
text: Given set fitted response curve models, find optimal spend allocation. Supports point-estimate optimization (fast, single solution) posterior-sampling optimization (slower, returns distribution solutions reflecting Bayesian uncertainty).

title: Optimize media mix allocation across channels — opt_mix
code: opt_mix(   mrms,   method = c("point", "posterior"),   objective = "max_kpi",   budget = NULL,   n_weeks = 1,   constraints = NULL,   bounds_multiplier = 3,   n_draws = 200,   seed = NULL,   parallel = FALSE,   xtol_rel = 1e-08,   maxeval = 1000,   verbose = TRUE )

title: Optimize media mix allocation across channels — opt_mix
text: mrms named list `mrmfit` objects (one per channel). method One `"point"` (default) `"posterior"`. Point uses posterior median parameters; posterior optimizes multiple MCMC draws. objective One `"max_kpi"` (default): maximize total KPI given budget constraint. budget Total budget period. `NULL` (default), inferred total current weekly spend across channels. n_weeks Number weeks budget covers. Used convert period budget weekly optimization. Default `1` (budget already weekly). Convenience values: use `52` annual, `13` quarterly, `4` monthly. constraints data frame per-channel bounds. Must contain columns `channel`, `min_spend`, `max_spend`. `NULL` (default), constraints auto-generated model return rate ranges. bounds_multiplier `constraints` `NULL`, multiplier applied auto-detected spend ranges. Default `3`. n_draws Number posterior draws optimize `method = "posterior"`. Default `200`. seed Random seed draw sampling. Default `NULL`. parallel Logical; use `future.apply` parallel posterior optimization. Default `FALSE`. Requires `future::plan()` set. xtol_rel Relative tolerance nloptr. Default `1e-8`. maxeval Maximum nloptr evaluations per solve. Default `1000`. verbose Print progress information. Default `TRUE`.

title: Optimize media mix allocation across channels — opt_mix
text: `opt_mix_result` S3 object. methods return   top-level structure: `$solution` — tibble one row per channel containing current       optimal spend, KPI, units, cost-per, response rate, share       columns. Posterior results include `_lower`/`_upper` CI columns. `$constraints` — tibble: channel, lb, ub, x0. `$budget_info` — list: total_budget, weekly_budget, n_weeks,       current_weekly. `$method` — `"point"` `"posterior"`. `$mrms` — named list `mrmfit` models. Point-fields: `$nloptr_result`, `$response_funs`.    Posterior-fields: `$draws_matrix`, `$kpi_matrix`,   `$solution_draws`, `$n_draws`, `$draw_ids`. Use [print()] [summary()] formatted console summary,   [opt_table()] tidy comparison tibble, [plot()]   standalone `opt_plot_*` functions visualizations.
